title: The Provenance Flip
phenomenon: A person's experience of an unchanged artwork can change after learning that it was human-made, AI-generated, forged, stolen, or produced without consent.
adversarialPrompt: Explain provenance-induced aesthetic reversal: how non-sensory knowledge about the origin, authorship, or ethics of an unchanged work could change what experiencing it feels like. Distinguish phenomenal change from a change in evaluation, decision, or report.
rationale: The sensory signal stays fixed while beauty, revulsion, meaning, trust, or emotional charge may reverse, forcing a theory to specify where top-down knowledge enters experience.
assumptions: The sensory stimulus is unchanged between the blind and provenance-reveal conditions.
The provenance information is understood and believed by the participant.
knownConfounds: Demand characteristics and social desirability can alter reports without altering experience.
Mislabelled provenance may change trust in the experimenter.
Memory of the first presentation may alter the second presentation.
targetCommitments: Whether non-sensory knowledge can alter phenomenal character.
Where authorship, origin, and ethical appraisal enter the proposed mechanism.
What observation separates phenomenal change from evaluation or report.

empiricalAnchors.label: MAC Lab #001 candidate by Kris Krüg

changeMindObservation: A preregistered blinded study repeatedly finds evaluation and report changes after provenance reveal but no discriminating pre-report experiential witness predicted by the theory.
